examples.title: Simple Turing Test Simulator
examples.description: Basic pseudocode demonstrating how a Turing Test evaluator might work
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TuringTestSimulator
BEGIN
  SET judgeScore = 0
  SET numRounds = 10
  
  FOR i = 1 TO numRounds DO
    DISPLAY "Round " + i + ": Ask a question to both contestants"
    question = INPUT
    
    responseFromHuman = getHumanResponse(question)
    responseFromMachine = getMachineResponse(question)
    
    randomizeOrder(responseFromHuman, responseFromMachine)
    
    DISPLAY "Response A: " + responseA
    DISPLAY "Response B: " + responseB
    
    guess = INPUT("Which is human? (A/B)")
    
    IF guess == correct THEN
      judgeScore = judgeScore + 1
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  IF judgeScore >= 6 THEN
    DISPLAY "Machine passes the Turing Test!"
  ELSE
    DISPLAY "Machine failed to convince the judge."
  END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode simulates a basic Turing Test setup where a judge asks questions to both a human and machine, then tries to identify which response came from the human. If the judge cannot reliably distinguish, the machine passes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of rounds

examples.title: Simple Chatbot Response Generator
examples.description: A basic Python implementation showing how a simple rule-based chatbot might generate responses for a Turing Test
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates a simple rule-based chatbot that could participate in a Turing Test conversation. It uses simple pattern matching to categorize inputs and generate responses, illustrating the basic principle of generating human-like text.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is length of user input for pattern matching
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing response patterns and state
